title: Statistical design offers a better way to plan complex experiments
text:

Scientists scheduling samples for advanced characterization analysis at the Johnson Matthey Technology Centre.Credit: Johnson MattheyA team of biochemical engineers is scaling up an enzyme-driven process to produce an essential pharmaceutical building block. The key parameters are well established at lab scale, but translating them to larger reactors proves challenging. Yields that are robust in small-scale experiments become highly sensitive to mixing, heat and mass transfer and feeding strategies, increasing waste and energy use under certain conditions. Although the system is well characterized, the interplay between temperature, substrate concentrations, solvent composition and biocatalyst loading shifts with scale, making it difficult to define operating windows that consistently deliver optimal performance. Beyond trial and error Until recently, such variability often led researchers to rely on traditional trial and error problem-solving methods to tackle scale-up challenges, adjusting one variable at a time in an effort to restore performance. Statistical approaches now offer a more systematic alternative, enabling multiple parameters to be explored simultaneously and revealing how key variables, and their interactions, shape outcomes. This shift is most commonly implemented through Design of Experiments (DOE), a framework that allows researchers to study multiple variables simultaneously. Pilar Gómez-Jiménez, a senior principal scientist at chemical company, Johnson Matthey, has applied DOE in her work for more than a decade. “It’s not just about running fewer experiments on a single problem,” she says. “The goal is to build models that can be predictive.” Such models can extend beyond technical performance to include estimates of cost and environmental impact, helping teams compare different options early on. In practice, Gómez‑Jiménez says, the method can reduce the amount of experimental work required later in a project. Tools for smarter experimentation JMP Statistical Discovery LLC, a statistical software company based in Cary, North Carolina, develops tools used to support DOE. Its software underpins the planning, analysis and visualisation of designed experiments, offering an alternative to one-factor-at-a-time approaches still common in laboratory research. “That’s like cutting holes in a door and trying to see into a room,” says Gómez‑Jiménez. “DOE lets you make the holes in the right places, so your view is as if the door was open. You can see clearly how factors interact and make decisions based on the data.” The approach is increasingly used in chemical and life-science research to accelerate experimental workflows and support earlier decision-making in development. By modelling multiple variables simultaneously, DOE can inform choices much earlier in a project, when changes are easiest, and cheapest, to make. “JMP offers a complete data science toolbox for scientists and engineers, combining data handling, visualization and machine-learning tools with modern experimental design methods such as Bayesian optimization,” says Phil Kay, a DOE expert at JMP Statistical Discovery. DOE is typically implemented through statistical software platforms that also include visualization features, which can help researchers interpret and communicate their results. “It means you can be impactful and compelling, with immediate, understandable graphics, rather than p‑values and model parameters,” Kay says. Gomez‑Jimenez says this reflects a broader shift in how experimental data are used. “I am a strong believer in the democratization of tools. This kind of software allows researchers to apply statistics to their own questions and their own fields. It’s the synergy between statistics and subject‑matter expertise.”
For more information on Design of Experiments and JMP’s approach, visit the website.
